description: Figure 1 comprises four panels comparing the annealed microstructures of Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steels. Panels (a) and (b) are optical micrographs, while (c) and (d) are accompanying schematic illustrations. Panel (a) shows a fully austenitic structure containing abundant annealing twins within equiaxed austenite grains, indicating fully recovered grains; the scale bar is 50 μm. Panel (b) reveals an austenite-based duplex microstructure, where elongated δ-ferrite grains are distributed in the γ-austenite matrix and aligned along the rolling direction (RD), with the normal direction (ND) indicated; the scale bar is 20 μm. Panels (c) and (d) schematically highlight the same morphological features for clarity: (c) depicts austenite grains intersected by parallel annealing twins, and (d) shows ferrite grains embedded in austenite grains. The figure collectively demonstrates that alloy design can stabilize either a single-phase austenitic matrix with profuse annealing twins or a duplex austenite-ferrite microstructure with elongated ferrite, providing the essential microstructural basis for the distinct mechanical responses of these low-density steels.

Figure 2 presents the isothermal phase sections of Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steels at 900 °C across three fixed Mn contents: a) 10 wt%, b) 20 wt%, and c) 30 wt%, with Al (wt%) on the abscissa and C (wt%) on the ordinate. The diagrams delineate single-phase and multiphase fields, where light gray, gold, and blue regions denote austenite (γ), ferrite (α), and κ-carbide, respectively; other labeled regions include γ+M₃C, γ+κ, γ+κ+α, α+κ, and B2+κ. As Mn increases from 10 wt% to 30 wt%, the austenite stability domain expands toward high-Al concentrations (>8 wt%), while the κ-phase field grows at high C (>3 wt%) and high Al (>10 wt%). Elevated Al concurrently promotes ferrite (α) formation, and C contents above approximately 1.2 wt% lead to M₃C and κ precipitation. Derived from CALPHAD calculations and experimental validation, these 900 °C isothermal sections are critical for industrial alloy design because this temperature is close to hot-rolling conditions, guiding composition selection for single-phase austenite or duplex microstructures.

This figure presents a time-temperature-transformation-style diagram illustrating the decomposition of the γ austenite phase in Fe–28Mn–8.5Al–1C–1.2Si (wt%) low-density steel during aging treatment over 0.01–1000 h and 300–1000 °C. Multiple C-shaped curves delineate distinct phase fields: single-phase γ at high temperature; fast-cooling regions producing γ+κ′; and slow-cooling regions progressing through γ+κ*, γ+κ*+α(B2/DO₃), and finally γ+κ+B2/DO₃. A dashed line marks the critical cooling rate of −4.4 °C·h⁻¹, separating fast and slow cooling pathways. The right side identifies ordered B2, B2+DO₃, and DO₃ stability fields. The diagram shows that κ-carbide precipitates from the austenite matrix when cooling is slower than −4.4 °C·h⁻¹, while faster cooling suppresses extensive decomposition. This supports the core conclusion that controlled cooling rate—via air-assisted spray cooling or furnace cooling—enables prediction and engineering of austenite decomposition and κ precipitation to improve mechanical properties in low-density steels.

This multi-panel figure combines schematics, TEM micrographs, and mechanical property plots to investigate deformation twinning and work-hardening in Fe–Mn–C–Al TWIP steels. Schematics (a) and (b) illustrate dislocations (yellow symbols) pinned by primary deformation twins (red bands labeled "Deformation twins") and the subsequent formation of secondary twins that further fragment the matrix. TEM images (c) and (d) provide direct nanoscale morphological evidence of deformation twins, with scale bars of 500 nm and 200 nm respectively; (d) shows intersecting twins and both insets contain selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns. Panel (e) plots the F/T ratio (fraction of twinned grains to twin thickness) versus true strain, revealing a steep increase for FeMnC steel while the FeMnCAl curve remains nearly flat. Panel (f) compares true stress and work-hardening rate versus true strain for 0Al and 1.5Al alloys. The caption and cited text interpret these results as evidence that deformation twins act as barriers to dislocation motion, reducing the dislocation mean free path, and that the markedly higher F/T in FeMnC correlates with stronger work hardening via a dynamic Hall–Petch effect relative to the Al-bearing counterpart.

This figure comprises three panels illustrating the work-hardening behavior of individual phases in Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steel and the Considère criterion for necking. Panels (a) and (b) plot effective plastic strain versus tensile flow stress (solid symbols) and work-hardening rate (open symbols). Panel (a) compares ferrite and bainite: bainite exhibits higher flow stress and hardening rate than ferrite, with marked necking strains of 0.127 for bainite and 0.163 for ferrite at the curve intersections. Panel (b) compares retained austenite and martensite: martensite shows substantially higher stress and hardening rate, with necking strains of 0.348 for austenite and 0.50 for martensite. Panel (c) is a schematic of the Considère criterion, indicating that the necking point occurs where the work-hardening rate equals the true stress, and that a higher work-hardening rate promotes higher uniform elongation and tensile strength. The caption states these single-phase responses were extracted via an ABAQUS/UMAT framework. Collectively, the data demonstrate that martensite possesses the strongest hardening capacity and the highest necking strain among the phases, supporting the core conclusion that martensite transformation in TRIP Fe–Mn–C–Al steel delays necking and enhances mechanical performance.

This figure presents multi-scale evidence linking aluminum content to dislocation slip mode transition and work hardening in Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steels. Panel (a) plots the parameter W_R (×10⁻⁴ m) versus Al content (wt.%), showing a linear decrease from approximately 29 at 0 Al to 10 at 6 Al, predicting a transition from wavy to planar slip. Corresponding TEM micrographs at ε=0.1 contrast the deformation substructures: (a1) for 3Al shows dislocation cells (DCs) characteristic of wavy slip, whereas (a2) for 6Al exhibits pronounced planar slip bands. Panel (b) provides an ECCI image at ε=0.14 revealing narrow, dense coplanar slip microbands. Panel (c) displays true stress and strain hardening rate versus true strain curves for the SRO-affected steel, illustrating the mechanical response and work hardening evolution. Collectively, the data demonstrate that increasing Al promotes planar dislocation glide, facilitates microband formation upon destruction of short-range order, and contributes to the dynamic Hall–Petch hardening and work hardening capacity in these steels.

This 3D plot illustrates the calculated relationship between yield-strength improvement (ΔσP, 0–900 MPa) and carbide size (0–100 nm) and volume fraction f (0.0–1.0 %) in Fe–Mn–C–Al steel. A continuous colored surface depicts the theoretical strengthening envelope, indicating that higher volume fractions combined with smaller carbide sizes produce markedly larger ΔσP. Overlaid cubic markers denote literature-reported precipitates: TiC [19], VC [21], two V(C,N) [19] variants (one at ~40 nm giving ~180 MPa and another at ~70–80 nm giving ~400 MPa), V4C3 [20] at ~50–60 nm (~300 MPa), VC/Fe3C [5] at ~70–80 nm (~280 MPa), and NbC [19] at ~80–90 nm with negligible strengthening. The visual trend confirms that nanoscale Ti-, V-, and Nb-based carbides serve as potent dislocation barriers, whereas large, low-fraction NbC contributes minimally. This supports the core conclusion that optimizing precipitate dispersion—maximizing volume fraction while minimizing particle size—is critical for strengthening Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steels via precipitation hardening.

Figure 8 presents the tribological behavior and worn-surface morphologies of Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steels under various aging treatments. Panel (a) plots the wear rate (×10⁻³ mm³/Nm) versus aging time for Fe–22.33Mn–1.35C–9.87Al aged at 500 °C, 550 °C, and 600 °C, showing a linear decrease with prolonged aging and higher temperatures; the as-homogenized condition starts at ~0.35. Panels (b) and (c) show SEM images of the worn surface and wear debris of the same alloy after 600 °C/3 h aging, revealing craters on the surface and irregular blocky debris, consistent with good wear resistance. Panels (d) and (e) shift to Fe–28Mn–1C–8.5Al quenched from 1050 °C and subsequently aged at 550 °C for 3 h and 4 h, respectively. The 3 h sample exhibits delamination, while the 4 h overaged surface shows broken coarse κ-carbide ((Fe, Mn)₃AlC) particles and associated microcracking along grain boundaries. Together, the figure demonstrates that optimal aging refines precipitates to improve wear resistance, whereas overaging coarsens κ particles, initiating cracks and delamination that degrade tribological performance.

Figure 9 consists of two scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs showing the microstructure of Fe–22Mn–0.4C–1.5Al–1.5Si (wt%) steel fabricated by metal injection molding (MIM) and sintered at 1360 °C. Subfigure (a) corresponds to 1 h sintering and subfigure (b) to 3 h. Both images reveal an equiaxed matrix explicitly labeled as austenitic, with dark manganese-oxide particles distributed along grain boundaries and at triple junctions, as annotated directly on the micrographs; each panel includes a 200 μm scale bar. Visual comparison indicates no obvious formation of additional phases when extending the hold time from 1 h to 3 h, and the oxide particles remain similarly dispersed, consistent with the caption’s report of a fully austenitic structure with equally distributed manganese oxides. This figure supports the conclusion that MIM sintering at 1360 °C produces a single-phase austenitic Fe–Mn–C–Al steel bearing dispersed manganese oxides, providing a microstructural basis for subsequent incorporation of ultrahard ceramic particles to enhance wear resistance.

Figure 10 presents a schematic road map illustrating the development strategy for Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steel. The diagram employs a flowchart format with three sequential nodes connected by a dotted pathway, depicting the progression from alloy design to heat treatment and final material objectives. The first node, labeled 'Alloyings', identifies three key design parameters: steel density, precipitation behavior, and strengthening mechanisms. The second node, 'Annealing & aging treatments', highlights the microstructural evolution processes including phase change and precipitation growth. The final node, 'Low density steel', summarizes the target performance attributes: lower density, higher strength, wear resistance, and low material cost. This roadmap synthesizes the review's central thesis that rational alloying combined with controlled annealing and aging treatments governs microstructural features such as austenite stability, κ-carbide precipitation, and duplex-phase formation, thereby enabling the optimization of lightweight Fe–Mn–C–Al steels for structural applications in automotive, aerospace, and mining industries.
